data.title: Abercrombie & Fitch: Weaker Times May Be Ahead (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Abercrombie & Fitch Co. (ANF) reported the company’s fiscal Q3 results from the August-October period on the 25th of November. The clothing retailer’s overall earnings performance remained good, split between a strong Hollister brand and moderation in Abercrombie’s brand relevance. The market took Abercrombie’s Q3 report very positively, sending the stock up nearly 38% for the day. I note a more mixed view of the report, highlighting future concerns.

I maintained a Buy rating in my previous May article on the stock, titled “Abercrombie & Fitch: Hollister Shines In Q1”. The stock has since returned 8%, still underperforming the S&P 500’s 15% gain amid industry-wide tariff worries.

Abercrombie’s Brand Performance Is Split as Fashion Trends Continue to Shift

Abercrombie’s performance has been split between the company’s two major brands, Hollister and Abercrombie. The most recent Q3 report again underlines the fact – Hollister showed massive 15% comparable store sales growth, while at the same time, the Abercrombie brand’s comparable store sales showed a very sharp -7% drop. Blended comparable growth still came in at 3%, and total revenue growth reached 7% through store openings. The $1.29 billion topline beat Wall Street’s consensus by $12 million—overall revenue momentum has remained fairly good for now.

The performance highlights Hollister’s continued brand relevance gains, as revenue growth for the brand has remained at a near industry-leading level. Considering Hollister’s scale, the growth is highly impressive. Growth is only gradually slowing from Q2 and Q1 but still underlines Hollister’s continued brand relevance gains that Google Trends and previous surveys also suggest. Year-on-year growth in Google search volume has continued into November as well, suggesting that Hollister’s growth should persist in Q4 as well. Hollister’s Y2K capsule, Crocs (CROX) collaboration, and other assortments have drawn the attention of Gen Z.

The Abercrombie brand has shown very clear moderation. After the brand had a highly impressive run in the FY2021-FY2024 period, the fashion cycle has now reversed, weakening Abercrombie’s consumer interest. Abercrombie is currently trying to attract an ongoing Western fashion trend with a Kemo Sabe collaboration, as the brand has a very agile lookbook, but Google Trends shows a mixed picture for Abercrombie’s relevance. Abercrombie’s sales decline did moderate from the first half of FY2025, but Q3 still highlights that the brand pendulum is swinging the other way. The company has made significant brand marketing investments in Abercrombie as selling expenses grew by 9% to $460 million in Q3, but such investments haven’t stabilized Abercrombie yet.

The company aims for Abercrombie to achieve flat net sales in Q4, achieved through continued store openings but moderating comparable sales. As comparable store sales growth is a critical factor in maintaining margins in retail, I believe that the Abercrombie brand’s outlook is now slightly concerning. A long winning streak has come to its end, shifting the question to when and where the brand’s sales will stabilize.

I believe that as the split between Hollister and Abercrombie continues, there’s cause for concern regarding future growth. Total revenue growth is guided to already decelerate to 4-6% in Q4, and there is likely more deceleration to come in FY2026 and beyond. Abercrombie will need to stabilize sales at some point, but after a hot multi-year streak, the brand's sales may end up considerably lower than currently. While Hollister still hasn’t lost momentum and seems likely to grow in FY2026 as well, similar deceleration may ultimately be ahead for Hollister as well as teens find an alternative brand. Most brands won’t stay hot forever, as Nike’s (NKE) struggles that I wrote on also underline. Having relevant brands is critical to maintaining high margins in apparel retail.

Tariffs Add Short-Term Earnings Pressure

Abercrombie has now started to struggle with tariffs as tariffed inventory has started to sell. Tariffs caused 210 basis points in gross margin pressure in Q3 already, and combined with increased selling expenses, the operating margin declined by 280 basis points to 12.0%. Operating income was still healthy at $155 million but declined noticeably from $179 million a year ago. Similarly, the Q4 operating margin is guided at around 14%, down by 220 basis points from 16.2% a year ago. The Q4 assumption reflects a more stark 360 basis point tariff headwind.

The longer-term tariff impact likely won’t be as significant. Gradual pricing increases and supply chain changes should start to mitigate the tariff impact from FY2026 forward. Ultimately, the long-term earnings outlook is more dependent on brand health.

ANF Stock’s Valuation Is Still Cheap, But I Suggest Caution

I updated my discounted cash flow (DCF) model for Abercrombie. I have weakened my earnings estimates—the mixed long-term brand growth outlook and tariffs add pressure to Abercrombie’s results.

After growth should still be very healthy in FY2026 as Hollister’s momentum is strong, I estimate the company to go through a period of weaker growth in FY2027-FY2030. Moderating sales with Abercrombie, and likely ultimately with Hollister as well, seems likely to cause such weakness as previously hot brand relevance moderates. I still estimate growth to stabilize at 2%.

Lower sales’ impact will likely be noteworthy on Abercrombie’s margins; while tariffs should mostly be mitigated, lower comparable sales are likely to push down earnings. I estimate the EBIT margin to stabilize at 11.5%, still suggesting very healthy earnings. Abercrombie’s cash flow conversion should remain good after taxes.

The estimates put Abercrombie’s fair value at $120.7, 27% above the stock price at the time of writing. The stock has considerable upside even when estimating and moderating earnings over the mid-term. Given uncertainty, especially around the Abercrombie brand, I still believe that the stock should be approached with caution—an even steeper earnings drop is possible.

The fair value estimate is down from $140.5 previously.

CAPM

The DCF model uses a cost of capital [WACC] of 9.51%, derived from a capital asset pricing model:

I don’t assume Abercrombie to draw debt. For cost of equity, I use the 10-year bond yield of 4.00% as the risk-free rate. The equity risk premium of 4.62% is Professor Aswath Damodaran’s most recent July estimate for the US. I have kept the beta estimate at 1.15. With a liquidity premium of 0.2%, the cost of equity and WACC both stand at 9.51%.

Takeaway

Abercrombie’s Q3 results sent the stock surging, but I highlight a more mixed long-term view. The company’s performance is split between brands, as Abercrombie’s relevance is moderating, but Hollister’s momentum remains strong. Ultimately, both brands face the fashion trend risk in upcoming years, of which Abercrombie has already shown major signs in FY2025. Tariffs add some additional pressure, albeit I believe tariffs’ margin impact to mostly be transitory.

ANF stock still has considerable upside if sales hold relatively stable, but I highlight increased caution - fashion trends are tough to predict, and Abercrombie hasn’t shown clear signs of stabilization yet. I downgrade my rating to Hold for now.

data.title: Personalis: A Growth Inflection Is Imminent, Hold
data.description:
Personalis (PSNL) is a liquid biopsy company that is initially targeting the MRD and drug research markets. While competition in these markets is fierce, Personalis is differentiated by its whole genome sequencing approach, which should benefit from declining sequencing costs.

The company is expected to begin gaining market share now that its test has started to receive Medicare coverage. The company's relationship with Tempus also provides significant distribution capabilities.

Investor sentiment has fluctuated in recent months, with the market weighing Personalis' poor current financial performance against its strong future prospects. While the stock surged on the back of the Medicare breast cancer coverage news, Personalis' valuation is probably still too low, although there is uncertainty around commercial adoption at scale and the competitive landscape longer term.

Medicare coverage announcements could continue to drive the stock higher in coming months, which should be supported by strong growth in 2026. Caution is probably warranted, though, as Personalis' Q4 results will likely still be poor and expectations have increased significantly.

Market

Personalis competes in the MRD (Minimal Residual Disease) cancer diagnostic market, which presents a relatively large opportunity (expected to exceed 20 billion USD per annum) that is still growing rapidly. This is a competitive market that is led by the likes of Roche, Labcorp and Natera (NTRA), with companies like Guardant Health (GH) and Exact Sciences (EXAS) also entering the market. In terms of blood-based testing, Natera is the clear market leader, although competition is rapidly increasing.

Within MRD, Personalis is differentiated by its use of whole genome sequencing. This approach potentially boosts performance but is more expensive. Declining DNA sequencing costs could make this a non-issue longer term, though. For example, Ultima is targeting 80 USD per genome, which would make sequencing costs relatively unimportant.

Personalis isn't the only company pursuing this approach, though. SAGA Diagnostics is developing an MRD platform that utilizes whole genome sequencing and multiplex digital PCR. Its flagship product (Pathlight) is a tumor-informed multi-cancer MRD test and is now available for commercial use in the US for breast cancer. The assay has also been submitted for US reimbursement via the MolDX Program. The clinical validity of Pathlight was established in the TRACER study, with data published in early 2025. This was a retrospective analysis of 100 patients with stage I-III breast cancer. The study demonstrated 100% sensitivity and specificity and a 13.7-month lead time to recurrence across Stage I-III breast cancer. Pathlight also demonstrated sensitivity below the 1 ppm level.

Guardant and Exact are also introducing more sensitive tests that appear set to bring performance more in line with Personalis' assay. Guardant Reveal Ultra is a tissue-informed MRD assay that is targeting 1 ppm level sensitivity. This is important given that sensitivity remains a challenge for tissue-informed MRD tests. Exact's MAESTRO technology leverages a whole genome sequencing approach and will be introduced in 2026. Exact is also targeting 1 ppm sensitivity and believes that its test will have a cost advantage.

Personalis

Personalis is a cancer diagnostics company that was founded in 2011. While Personalis provides a range of solutions across areas like therapy selection, and patient stratification and clinical trial monitoring for biopharma partners, its focus is on early recurrence detection for cancer survivors.

Personalis is differentiated by its whole genome sequencing and use of large personalized panels (up to 1,800 variants). The NeXT Personal Dx LDT is a blood-based test for MRD that was launched in October 2023. It is a tumor-informed assay that offers significantly higher sensitivity than competing approaches (approximately 1 part per million with 99.9% specificity), which should help Personalis to detect cancer recurrence sooner. Around 40% of Personalis' results are in the ultrasensitive range, giving the company confidence in negative results and highlighting the importance of sensitivity.

The efficacy of the NeXT Personal test is supported by strong MRD results for breast cancer, lung cancer, and immunotherapy monitoring. For example, NeXT Personal detected breast cancer ~15 months before imaging in one study. Personalis' test was also able to detect cervical cancer progression up to 16 months ahead of imaging in an AstraZeneca study.

The company's initial focus has been on breast cancer, lung cancer, and immunotherapy monitoring. Personalis has been expecting results to be published in medical journals, which will support submissions for Medicare reimbursement. This includes studies for breast cancer (Royal Marsden), therapy monitoring (VHIO), and lung cancer (TRACERx). Manuscripts from all 3 studies have now been accepted, enabling Personalis to submit for Medicare reimbursement.

TRACERx was a study of early-stage non-small cell lung cancer, with patients stratified post-operation. Personalis was able to detect lung cancer 6-11 months ahead of standard imaging and ahead of other assays. Test results were also predictive of 5-year RFS and OS. This suggests that Personalis' test can be used to identify high- and low recurrence-risk patients and improve treatment decisions relative to the current standard of care.

Immunotherapy and lung cancer were recently submitted for Medicare coverage, and Personalis is confident that data will be sufficient for coverage. Personalis recently received Medicare coverage for its MRD test in breast cancer. Medicare beneficiaries with stage I and II breast cancer are covered for up to 6 years of recurrence monitoring. Personalis previously stated that it was on track to receive Medicare coverage for 2 indications by the end of the year, suggesting either IO monitoring or lung cancer will also receive coverage in the next few weeks.

Personalis is also conducting over 20 studies at the moment, with the goal of rapidly expanding the indications it has Medicare coverage for. This includes:

  • B‑STRONGER‑1 prospective breast cancer study
  • A 100-patient early-stage breast cancer study with Dana-Farber and another with MD Anderson.
  • A number of immunotherapy studies, covering areas like melanoma, gastric cancer, and lung cancer.
  • The CATE trial, which was announced in September. It aims to determine whether monitoring for recurrence in breast cancer patients, and preemptive treatment with elacestrant can prevent relapse.

The focus of investors is now likely to shift to end market demand and Personalis' sales and marketing efforts. The company is well positioned in this regard as it has partnered with Tempus, and is leveraging its ~200-person sales force. Tempus reps are now commercializing NeXT Personal across 4 indications (breast, lung, and colorectal cancer and immunotherapy monitoring). Personalis is also expanding its own sales force and expects to have 12-15 field professionals by the end of the year. While the long-term viability of the relationship is unclear, the interests of the two companies are aligned by Tempus' 35 million USD investment in Personalis.

Personalis also offers ImmunoID NeXT, a tumor profiling platform that supports research. This solution is targeted at biopharma companies developing immunotherapies, and has already been adopted by more than 50 partners across targeted therapies, cell therapies, and personalized cancer vaccines. Personalis plans on broadening its product offering for biopharma companies, and there is also potential for Personalis' products to become companion diagnostics.

ImmunoID NeXT also powers personalized vaccine design (based on neoantigen prediction and selection), with NeXT Personal enabling patient-specific monitoring of therapy response. Neoantigens are only present in cancer cells, and Personalis' platform can identify these neoantigens so that individualized therapies can be created. Personalis has provided its tumor profiling capabilities to Moderna in support of its efforts to create personalized neoantigen therapies. The companies have partnered from clinical trials through to commercialization in a 10-year commercial deal.

Financial Analysis

While Personalis' revenue growth has been weak in recent years, it is important to understand that there is a large shift occurring in the business at the moment. Biopharma has dominated revenue in the past, like Moderna's Phase 3 melanoma trial, which boosted revenue during the first three quarters of 2024. Personalis has also lost the majority of Natera's business (~20 million USD), negatively impacting growth in 2025. Personalis' partnership with Natera started in 2021, with Personalis providing data to help validate the Signatera assay. This helped Personalis with early commercialization but Natera is now bringing the relevant services in-house. Volumes related to the VA MVP have also fluctuated quarter to quarter.

Personalis generated 17.2 million USD revenue in Q2, down 24% YoY. Roughly 5.6 million USD of the decline was related to Natera, while 1.3 million USD was attributed to Moderna. While clinical revenue was only 0.5 million USD, the core clinical business has been exceeding expectations. Biopharma revenue was roughly 11 million USD, which was attributed to project delays. Despite current headwinds, Personalis remains confident in this part of its business, and its NeXT Personal funnel continues to grow.

Personalis generated 14.5 million USD revenue in the third quarter, a 44% decline YoY. While this result appears bad, much of the decline was due to Natera (4.6 million USD) and the VA MVP (4.2 million USD). Biopharma customers were also responsible for 2.5 million USD of the decline.

Personalis delivered 4,388 clinical tests in Q3, a 26% sequential increase. There were in excess of 700 physicians ordering the NeXT Personal test in Q3. In comparison, NeXT Personal clinical test volume totaled almost 3,500 in Q2, up 59% sequentially. There were over 600 physicians ordering the test in the quarter, with Personalis receiving around 1,750 USD per test.

Personalis initially expected 80-90 million USD revenue in 2025 but is now only anticipating 68-73 million USD revenue in 2025, implying roughly 6% YoY growth in the fourth quarter. Personalis has been targeting 30-40% sequential revenue growth until reimbursement is achieved. Growth is likely to accelerate meaningfully now that reimbursement has been received, though. Personalis also recently signed 2 major prospective clinical trials that will be serviced over the next 2-3 years, and new biopharma customers remain on track to generate 5 million USD revenue in 2025.

In addition, Personalis was recently awarded work worth up to 13.5 million USD by the Department of Veterans Affairs (4). The new task order was expected to take effect at the end of September, meaning there could be a boost starting in the fourth quarter. The VA is a material contributor to Personalis' top line in recent years, contributing around 9% of revenue in 2024. The company has now received almost 225 million USD in task orders from the VA.

Personalis' gross margin was roughly 28% in Q2, with the YoY decline attributed to lower revenue and unreimbursed clinical test costs. Excluding unreimbursed clinical test costs, Personalis' gross margin would have been roughly 40%.

The company's gross margin declined to 13.2% in Q3 due to a combination of lower fixed cost absorption and increased clinical test costs in advance of reimbursement. Excluding those expenses, Personalis' gross margin would have been approximately 31%.

Gross margins are expected to move above 50% once reimbursement coverage is received for multiple indications. Longer term, Personalis is targeting 60% gross margins, with laboratory automation helping to drive margin expansion.

Personalis' operating expenses totaled 26.6 million USD in Q2, with the increase driven by sales and marketing, while free cash flow was -13.2 million USD. Operating expenses were 25.2 million USD in the third quarter, with the YoY increase attributed to selling expenses as clinical test volume ramps. Free cash flow in the third quarter deteriorated to -23.4 million USD.

While Personalis' losses are sizeable at the moment, this situation should change rapidly over the next few quarters now that Medicare coverage has been received. This is supported by the scalability that the Tempus relationship provides. Personalis pays Tempus the fair-market value of sales and marketing, which is expected to be around 20-25% of sales. As a result, I can see the company's operating margins trending towards 20-25% as the burden of R&D investments eases.

Personalis ended Q3 with roughly 151 million USD cash and short-term investments and minimal debt. This is expected to be sufficient for the company to reach cash flow breakeven. This appears reasonable based on the company's recent cash burn rate, although it will ultimately depend on how quickly sales ramp.

Conclusion

Personalis' stock has performed well over the past 12 months, which has been driven by a combination of undervaluation and material progress in the underlying business. Blood-based cancer diagnostics is generally an area that has been deeply undervalued, though, with most stocks performing well in 2025 as the potential of the screening, MRD, and precision oncology markets became better understood. Personalis remains something of an outlier relative to peers as its enterprise value is low and it is at a point of revenue inflection.

Investors are hoping that Personalis can replicate Natera's success. This could be difficult, though, as Natera has a commanding lead, and competition will likely narrow Personalis' technology lead over time. Even with more modest success, Personalis could still handsomely reward investors going forward. Analysts are currently expecting around 165 million USD revenue in 2029, although this estimate is likely to prove too low.

Personalis is competing in a 20 billion USD market that is still largely up for grabs. As a result, I would not be surprised if the company is generating close to 1 billion USD in revenue within a decade, leading to mid- to high-teen annual returns for investors.

Personalis' ability to capitalize on the MRD opportunity is dependent on both its sales and marketing capabilities and its ability to maintain a technology lead. Personalis' low detection limits seem to lead to strong test performance, but competitors are in the process of introducing similar capabilities. Larger peers also have access to more data and have infrastructure in place that allows them to utilize healthcare system data to expand coverage of their tests. While Personalis could have bright prospects, it is not clear to me how this situation is going to unfold over the long term.

data.title: Columbia Pyrford International Stock Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Fund performance

  • Columbia Pyrford International Stock Fund Institutional Class shares returned 2.57% for the quarter ending September 30, 2025.
  • The fund's benchmark, the MSCI EAFE Index-Net, returned 4.77% for the same period.
  • Please check online at columbiathreadneedleus.com for monthly performance.

Market overview

During the third quarter of 2025, most major asset classes delivered positive returns, driven by a combination of easing trade tensions, heightened enthusiasm around artificial intelligence (AI) and growing expectations for near-term interest-rate cuts by the U.S. Federal Reserve. With tariff passthrough proving less severe than initially feared, the Fed responded by cutting interest rates by 25 basis points (BPS) at its September meeting — its first rate reduction of the year. (A basis point is 1/100 of one percent.) This move signalled a potential shift toward further monetary easing, which in turn provided support for U.S. equities. Meanwhile, the European Central Bank opted to keep rates unchanged in September, indicating that it is approaching the end of its current easing cycle.

Quarterly portfolio recap

U.S. stocks posted strong gains during the quarter, with the Magnificent Seven stocks (Alphabet (GOOGL), Amazon (AMZN), Apple (AAPL), Meta Platforms (META), Microsoft (MSFT), NVIDIA (NVDA) and Tesla (TSLA)) leading the rally. This was driven by anticipation and delivery of a Fed rate cut, strong corporate earnings, especially within technology and continued enthusiasm over AI. The fund's relative underperformance was mainly explained by our underweight and stock selection in the eurozone and stock selection in Australia.

Top holdings exclude short-term holdings and cash, if applicable. Fund holdings are as of the date given, are subject to change at any time, and are not recommendations to buy or sell any security. Current and future bond holdings are subject to risk.

In Europe, stock selection within the eurozone detracted. In Germany, Fuchs Petrolub, a global producer and distributor of lubricants, lagged, reflecting a weaker macro environment globally during the second quarter. Tariff uncertainties in the U.S., along with increasing input costs, were cited as the key reasons for subdued customer demand. The company is positioned well for growth in EV market exposure and has had continued dividend growth. In the Netherlands, Wolters Kluwer underperformed, due to worries around how AI could disrupt the business model for information services. In particular, a portion of Wolters Kluwer revenue comes from UpToDate, the leading clinical decision support tool for physicians globally. OpenEvidence, a new competitor founded in 2021, has emerged as a potential threat to this business, and has seen rapid adoption as a viral hit in the U.S. The competitive advantage for Wolters Kluwer's UpToDate comes from combining deep data sets with proprietary information that is edited and checked for accuracy before being integrated into the workflow of professionals. This means UpToDate moves beyond data gathering to a productivity tool that can become essential for the professional.

In Switzerland, Nestle's underperformance during the quarter followed a weak update on volumes and mix at the half-year results. Most growth came from price increases that reflected higher commodity costs across coffee and confectionery categories. Unexpected news of a CEO change also distracted from the story of execution of the turnaround strategy.

In Australia, stock selection detracted. Woolworths performed poorly as their results released in August disappointed the market. It became clear that management time spent on various regulatory and political inquiries during 2024 and 2025 had been at the expense of focus on Woolworths' core food business. After a recent meeting with the company, we are satisfied that this situation will be remedied, and performance should begin to recover. QBE Insurance Group (OTCPK:QBIEY) showed very strong performance results for the half year, with both net profit and interim dividends up. However, the company's outlook commentary was not as confident as the market was hoping for, and the stock fell sharply on the day of the results.

In Japan, our underweight detracted but stock selection added. The following companies contributed to positive performance: Nabtesco reported improving orders for precision reduction gears, a key product for the company. Orders increased on the previous year. Mitsubishi Electric benefited from general strength in cyclical products, with its factory automation segment impacted by the same improving trend seen by Nabtesco. While a small segment, defense orders also grew and benefited from a change in government policy to allow higher margins at domestic defense contractors. Nissan Chemical (OTCPK:NNCHY) has various products in the semiconductor material space, and an improving outlook for semiconductors in general was seen as positive for material producers.

Stock selection in Hong Kong added to relative returns as well. One key contributor was ASM Pacific Technology (OTCPK:ASMVY), which helped returns following second-quarter results that were well received. The company's third-quarter guidance was also above expectations on sustained advanced packaging growth. ASM Pacific is a key beneficiary of the AI theme, given the increased demand for advanced packaging tools.

On a sector basis, our underweight to and stock selection in financials detracted. Stock selection in materials also detracted. Our stock selection in health care and communication services added. Sector positioning is a residual of the opportunities we find at the country level.

Portfolio changes

Within the eurozone, we initiated a position in Daimler Truck, funded by a reduction in Brenntag, DHL Group, Fuchs and Sanofi (SNY). We also increased our position in L'Oreal (OTCPK:LRLCY). Daimler Truck, spun off from the car division in 2021, is one of the top four truck manufacturers that collectively hold 80% of the market in Europe and the U.S. Daimler Truck leads the U.S. heavy-duty truck segment, with a 40% market share. The company has also been actively repurchasing shares. Given its valuation and positioning, we view the stock's total return potential as compelling at this stage of the truck cycle.

Outlook

Policy uncertainty remains high, especially around tariffs and fiscal sustainability. Global growth may stabilize, but risks from trade tensions, geopolitical conflicts and inflation remain elevated. Some indices are at or near record highs, and this can lead to valuation concerns among market participants. In addition, there is still the potential for inflation resurgence or reports of slower-than-expected growth numbers, both of which could cool the markets.

The portfolio remains defensively positioned, with an underweight to quantitative-easing-fueled markets and an overweight to Southeast Asian markets. The European portfolio is concentrated in the U.K., Switzerland and the relatively healthy economies of core Europe. Economies in Southeast Asia offer sustainable economic growth, supported by increased labor output, or productivity growth, and trade at more reasonable valuations. We remain underweight to Japan, as its potential economic growth rate remains low, given its poor demographics and low productivity growth.

In terms of sectors, we have limited exposure to more cyclical sectors such as financials and consumer discretionary. Overweight positions are held in defensive sectors such as industrials and communications. These sectors offer predictable revenue streams and attractive valuations. The focus of the portfolio is on balance-sheet strength, profitability, earnings visibility and value.

As of quarter-end on September 30, 2025, the portfolio had no currency hedges in place.

data.title: Pool Corporation: Stabilizing But Not Recovering
data.description:
Shares of Pool Corporation (POOL) have been a very poor performer over the past year, losing 37% of their value. The company has struggled with a persistently weak pool construction market as consumers defer expensive discretionary purchases. With an elevated valuation, shares had little room for a prolonged slowdown, leading to significant underperformance. I last covered shares of Pool Corporation in May when I reiterated the stock as a “sell,” and since then, shares have lost 22% of their value, justifying my bearishness. With updated financials and shares approaching my $225 price target, now is a good time to revisit POOL.

Pool Corp’s business has been negatively impacted by a severe downturn in construction activity. As you can see below, its business surged post-COVID. Housing turnover was high, interest rates were low, and consumers were spending more around their houses, given remote and hybrid work. Earnings basically tripled from 2019 to 2022. Since then, we have seen earnings fall by ~40%. A sharp Fed rate hiking cycle has made borrowing to fund large projects more costly, while years of elevated inflation have sapped consumers’ savings and willingness to spend. Plus, some of the 2021-2022 construction activity likely represented a pull-forward of future demand that has since normalized.

It is important to remember that Pool’s business is not as cyclical as new pool construction. The majority of the business is tied to ongoing maintenance and repair work. While some activities can be deferred, others, like chemical treatments, cannot be avoided and provide a steady stream of revenue. New construction was only 14% of the business last year, with renovation and remodel work another 22%. Essentially, 36% of the business is highly cyclical, while 64% is fairly steady. It is this cyclical piece that has dragged down results.

Even with this diversity, results continue to be muted. In the company’s third quarter, reported on October 23rd, Pool Corp. earned $3.39, which beat estimates by $0.02 as revenue grew 1% to $1.5 billion. Gross margins expanded 50 bps in Q3 to 29.6%. As a result, gross profit grew about 3% to $429 million. Offsetting this, operating expenses as a share of sales rose by 50 bps to 17.3%. Accordingly, operating margins were stable at 12.3%. In its key states, sales in California and Arizona were down 3%, while Texas was flat, showing some stability after a period of meaningful underperformance.

As you can see below, POOL has been raising prices of non-commodity items in order to recoup tariff costs, providing about a 2% tailwind to revenue. However, this was partially offset by lower volumes. Maintenance work was actually up slightly from a year ago, but discretionary activity was down 2% as consumers are reluctant to make a major purchase like a pool, especially in this elevated interest rate environment.

Notably, building materials spending rose for the first time in three years, admittedly off of a low base. Still, we may be nearing stabilization here. Equipment sales are also positive. While some maintenance work can be deferred for a time, it cannot be deferred forever. After several years of weak activity, there may be more quasi-discretionary maintenance that no longer can be delayed, helping to provide some support for the business.

On the last earnings call, management said that consumer sentiment is more optimistic than it was, though it expects more rate cuts will be necessary to boost demand. This is especially true among “entry-level” buyers. In general, we have seen higher-income consumption hold up better than at the low-end, a phenomenon increasingly referred to as a “K-shaped” economy. While the Fed is cutting rates, I believe we need to see long-term rates push below 6% to meaningfully alter construction-related activity.

Turning to its balance sheet, POOL is carrying $128 million of cash and $1.1 billion of debt. As such, debt/EBITDA leverage stands at 1.6x. This is a reasonable level, given the mix of the business, though it is up from 1.41x a year ago. Inventory is up 4% from last year to $1.2 billion, partially reflecting the higher cost of goods due to tariffs.

So far this year, the company has generated $237 million of free cash flow, but this included ~$134 million of working capital headwinds. Otherwise, free cash flow would be closer to $370 million. I expect some of this working capital pressure to reverse in Q4, and the company should end the year with ~$350-$375 million of free cash flow, or $9-10/share. The company has repurchased $164 million of stock so far this year. Thanks to buybacks, its share count is down about 2.3% from last year. Shares also offer a 2.1% dividend yield.

Alongside results, the company reiterated guidance. I would note that POOL’s outlook includes $0.11 of one-time tax benefits. Back in May, I saw the company earning $11.00-$11.30. Results are not materially different than my outlook but are slightly lower at the midpoint. I expect POOL to earn ~$11 this year. My outlook drove my $225 target as I struggled to value POOL at more than 20x earnings, given my view that a sharp rebound in demand was unlikely.

To me, it does appear that Pool’s results are near, or at, their trough. After a multiyear contraction, activity is sufficiently low that it is simply unlikely to get much worse, absent a recession. While there has been some softening in the labor market, I do expect the US to avoid recession. As such, I do not expect Pool’s earnings to really fall below the ~$11 level, and we should see free cash flow improve in 2025 due to a deferred tax payment this year.

That said, I do not see a recipe for a V-shaped recovery off of this level. Labor markets are tepid, and discretionary income growth is muted. I expected housing starts and existing home sales to continue to slow, a headwind for new construction activity, especially in the Sun Belt (the Florida housing market seems particularly weak). That said, POOL will benefit from deferred maintenance activity. I also expect it to continue gaining market share, as years of weak activity have likely pushed out some marginal players.

As such, I am looking for the company to earn $10.75-$11.75 next year, depending on construction activity. I believe much of the decline in shares this year has not been because 2025 was surprisingly weak but because investors have been ratcheting down expectations for the pace of recovery, causing POOL’s multiple to compress from elevated levels. Even today, shares are trading at ~21x my 2026 expectation, which is not cheap unless one is expecting a sharp recovery beyond 2026.

On this drop, POOL is definitely not a buy in my view. A meaningful recovery is still being priced in. I would want to see shares closer to 18x before I would actually be a buyer at $205-$210. That said, I am cautious about letting a winning trade run too long. Since I first rated POOL a sell in 2023, shares have underperformed the market by ~85 percentage points, after all. There has been tremendous underperformance as markets have gradually come toward my view that construction would be slow to recover.

I view ~20x earnings as a reasonable price target, reflecting the fact POOL has consistently enjoyed a premium multiple thanks to its dominant market position and solid balance sheet. This suggests a fair value of $230, about 6% below current levels. This leaves shares a close call between a sell and a hold in my view, as no fair value estimate is exact. There is not enough downside to merit being short.

On balance, I remain a “sell,” though it is less clear-cut than in May. I come to this conclusion in part because I see modest downside. More so though, I struggle to see a case for material upside from here. A sharp rebound in construction appears unlikely, with stabilization at muted levels more likely. As such, I see shares, at best, treading water around current levels, and investors should allocate their capital elsewhere to stocks that have a clearer opportunity for upside performance.

data.title: Waterdrop: Secular Growth And A Vast Pool Of Prospects
data.description:
Thesis

I'm rating Waterdrop Inc. (WDH) as Buy, expecting a 30% upside for the next 12 months, based on a residual income model backed by the following aspects.

First, the secular growth of the industry; second, the buybacks and dividends, which are combining for around 10% per year. Third, the vast user base on the crowdfunding platform, which represents one-third of the total population in China. This user base could be a great source for future and sustainable growth.

Lastly, regulation, competition, and the increased customer bargaining power are factors that require careful consideration.

Company Overview

Waterdrop Inc. is an insurtech (insurance technology) company in China that focuses on two key activities: an insurance marketplace and a crowdfunding platform. Insurance represents 88% of total revenues, and it's the only segment showing operating profit.

This last quarter was outstanding, both QoQ and YoY. Revenues are growing 24% Y/Y on a QoQ basis in RMB and 10.97% on a TTM Y/Y basis in USD. It's also interesting to note that not only revenues but also earnings grew 36% QoQ.

Regarding the crowdfunding segment, when we analyze it from a Business Canvas model perspective, it is a key activity because it successfully acquires prospects who are subsequently offered insurance policies. Although the crowdfunding segment accounts for just 8% of total revenues and has an operating loss, it contributes indirectly yet critically to the insurance sector. The total number of users who have donated using the platform is quite high, according to Corporate Secretary Jasmine Lee in the last earnings call.

As of the end of June 2025, around 480 million people cumulatively donated CNY 70 billion to about 3.54 million patients through our Waterdrop Medical Crowdfunding platform.

To put that into perspective, China has a population of around 1,414 million people, so this means that 33.94% (480/1414) of people in China have used the platform to donate.

The company is focusing strongly on developing and improving its AI models. They use these models to scan campaigns and comments to detect and prevent potential fraud. A campaign is the "profile and story" of a person with an illness who needs a donation to fund the treatment. Now their LLM detects 15 high-risk scenarios of potential fraud. This is crucial to building and maintaining a trustworthy image of the platform and the company's reputation. If there are a lot of fraudulent campaigns, it undermines the business model.

Regarding the Chinese insurtech industry, Grandview Research is very optimistic, expecting a 54.7% CAGR until 2030. Mordor Intelligence, on the other hand, forecasts a 13.81% CAGR for the broader insurance industry. Separately, Precedence Research expects a 37.1% CAGR by 2034 for the insurtech industry. It is clearly key to distinguish between traditional insurance and insurtech projections here. While Waterdrop is growing at 24% Y/Y, it didn't see that growth until the most recent quarter. It will be critical to follow up in the next quarters to determine if this monetization of the crowdfunding platform user base is sustainable, and to what extent.

They plan to repurchase shares worth up to $50 million through September 2026. This currently represents 8.3% of the market cap, which is significant. Additionally, the board of directors approved a cash dividend of US$10.9 million (1.8% of market cap and a dividend yield of 3.03%), which was paid on November 7.

As of today, November 23, Seeking Alpha's quant rating is a Strong Buy, with an index of 4.52. Growth metrics are above the industry median, and regarding valuation, it is more modest, but depending on the ratio that we'd choose, it would indicate some degree of undervaluation. Current profitability is subpar, but it could improve in the coming years due to growth in sales and, more importantly, in earnings. Lastly, earnings revisions show a clear upward trend since May 2025. In other words, Waterdrop is accelerating growth, especially in earnings.

The company is set to release earnings on December 3 (pre-market). Analysts are projecting $0.04 for non-GAAP quarterly earnings per share; in the last quarter, it was $0.03. For the topline, they expect $120mm in revenues for the quarter—a 2.56% bump from the last one (10.6% annualized).

Now, if we forecast using the last 11 months, there is a two-sided issue. The predicted value using the native Excel function to forecast for next quarter's revenue is $106 million. This is because the last quarter was an exceptional one, so statistical models may assume some "reversion to the mean". But on the other hand, the overall trend is upward, so a figure closer to the analyst's is also reasonable. While I'm not expecting sales to decline next quarter, it's worth mentioning that some statistical models point towards a mild dip ahead.

The main reasons that I believe this acceleration of the last two quarters may be sustainable, at least to some extent, are the following. First, there was a skyrocketing increase of 80% Y/Y of first-year premiums; this implies that most of that may be new clients. Second, AI implementation is helping match and convert 50% better than traditional advertisement slots. Third, since its rollout in March, the AI service customer agent has scaled rapidly, which boosts efficiency and prevents manual bottlenecks. Fourth, there was an expansion of the product portfolio. It's worth mentioning, for example, that a new product for people with pre-existing conditions contributed CNY 300 million, making up 9.36% of the total volume (CNY 300M/CNY 3,204M).

Wrapping up, this product portfolio expansion, combined with different AI implementations, supports this new trend of the last two quarters. Because of these new rollouts, I expect revenues to be at $120mm to $125mm for the 3Q 25.

Valuation

On a relative basis, Seeking Alpha's quant rating is "B," and when these ratios are compared to the median sector, they suggest a potential upside of 78% on average at first glance. Digging deeper, we see three aspects to consider. First, the most important ratio may be the P/E Non-GAAP forward; I will discuss it later in this section. Second, sales-based ratios suggest the most upside, especially EV-based ones. As a rule of thumb, enterprise value (EV) is more robust than market cap, as it considers cash and debt. Also, stockholders need earnings (or free cash flows) instead of just sales, because they take into account expenses. While sales can be useful for comparing companies in the early stages, in most cases, earnings-based ratios are more representative for valuation. That said, even after growing 24% Y/Y (which is not a bad figure), sales-based ratios are not expensive, but quite the opposite. In other words, other companies that are growing fast are trading at double or triple the sector median; this obviously is not the case. The third aspect to consider is to factor in growth. When a company is growing fast, forward ratios are going to differ from TTM-based ones, and the greater the growth and the farther out the years ahead, the bigger the difference.

Now, from a historical perspective, the price-to-book ratio indicates a fair value zone, and the EV/revenues suggests even overvaluation, as it is trading above the historical mean. This is why I consider it critical to use different valuation models and to avoid having a narrow perspective.

Analysts forecast earnings growing around 24% Y/Y, and when we consider, for example, the 2027 ratio, it suggests a significant upside ahead. Forward price ratios are as follows:

    • FY 2025: 10.33x

    • FY 2026: 8.46x

    • FY 2027: 6.25x

When it comes to absolute valuation, here is the scenario I am considering for Waterdrop. Earnings are growing at a 13.1% CAGR in the next 10 years. The discount rate is 11.15%, which is something on the high end of American companies, to take into account the increased risk for being a Chinese company.

While these assumptions might sound optimistic, I believe they are quite conservative. The company's earnings are growing around 70% Y/Y in both TTM and quarterly. Also, the payout rate has been 10.1%, due to the combination of dividends at 1.8% plus 8.3% of share buybacks. If the company maintains this policy, and it gets factored into the model, the potential upside would be 43% for the next 12 months, according to this model. Also, it's reasonable to expect an earnings growth rate above 13%; if the trend of the last quarter is sustained, the growth rate could be higher. However, I decided to remain conservative on both aspects, given the uncertainty and risks covered in the following section.

Risks

There are three key risks for Waterdrop: regulations, competition, and high dependency on brokerage.

The biggest risk for Waterdrop is the current and future potential regulations. The insurance industry is controlled by the China Banking and Insurance Regulatory Commission (CBIRC). There are three aspects to consider here. First, the CBIRC regulates digital distribution channels, which means it addresses data privacy concerns, misleading advertising cases, and the suitability of the product itself for the client. Second, brokerage commissions. In short, the CBIRC can limit commissions to lower consumer costs. Third, it regulates the insurance policy; a common problem in China was the confusion concerning the short-term healthcare insurance. People paid for an insurance policy with a one-year coverage term due to the lower price, but the single-year limitation was not adequately communicated to them by the sellers. These kinds of issues fueled the increasing regulatory waves by the Chinese Government.

Second, competition is fierce. The are two types of competitors for Waterdrop: traditional insurers and the insurtechs. Regarding the traditional insurers, I don't believe they are a big issue, but the insurtechs are a greater threat that could squeeze the growth and shrink the margins of Waterdrop. There are three key competitors to highlight. First, Ant Group of Alibaba (BABA); their main competitive advantage is that they have Alipay, which is the biggest payment app in China. Second, there is Tencent's WeChat Pay (OTCPK:TCEHY), which has the second biggest payment app. We should keep in mind that WeChat is the top instant messaging app in China (similar to Facebook Messenger, Apple Messages, or WhatsApp), but it is far more than just messages; it's a whole ecosystem, and its user base is huge. In other words, WeChat users are just a few clicks away from getting insurance. Lastly, there is the "Ping a good Doctor" company; this one is the health division of the largest Chinese insurance company called "Ping An Insurance". The two key factors of this last competitor are its large size and its high degree of vertical integration with hospitals and doctors.

In third place, most revenue comes from commissions paid by third-party insurance partners. Waterdrop sells insurance, but doesn't underwrite the policies, which poses two threats. First, insurance companies could suddenly change the fees/rates, affecting the revenues and margins of Waterdrop. Second, they could move to "cut out the middleman" by selling directly to the customers.

Additionally, setting aside the fundamentals, we also need to consider the liquidity and volume factor. This ADR has relatively low volume. Its 20-day average is 210,000 shares, which equates to a daily value of $569K in USD. As a rule of thumb, it is not a good idea to trade over 5% to 10% of this daily mean. Please be aware of this increased liquidity risk.

Conclusion

At first glance, it initially seemed like a Hold, mainly due to trading at historical average price/book levels, combined with regulatory hurdles and fierce competitors, which create uncertainty regarding future growth and margins. However, despite these headwinds, the company had an impressive last quarter, and if this trend continues, my assumptions (earnings growing at 13% CAGR) will fall short.

There is uncertainty related to factors such as stronger competition, further regulations, or a continued macroeconomic decline in China. That said, I stated what I believe is the most likely scenario in terms of projections. There are four drivers for my assumptions: secular industry growth, the acceleration in sales and earnings growth in the last quarter, the buybacks and dividends policy, and a vast pool of potential prospects, which represent one-third of the Chinese population. All of this changed my initial impression and brought me into the bull camp.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Northern Multi-Manager Global Listed Infrastructure Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
In the third quarter of 2025, the global listed infrastructure sector, as measured by the S&P Global Infrastructure Net Index, returned 3.50%. While utilities and airports were the top performing sectors, highways and rail tracks and independent power producers (IPPs) lagged during the quarter. From a regional perspective, Canada, Western Europe and the United States outperformed while the United Kingdom and Australia lagged.

The Fund underperformed its benchmark, the S&P Global Infrastructure Net Index, during the third quarter with a return of 2.60%. The primary drivers of the underperformance was an overweight to the United Kingdom, an underweight to and stock selection in Canada, and non-benchmark positioning in communications towers. Contributors to performance included stock selection in the United States, including non-benchmark exposures to U.S. railroads, and stock selection in highways and rail tracks. Sub-adviser Lazard (LAZ) underperformed due to an overweight in the United Kingdom and an underweight to Canada. Sub-adviser First Sentier Investors outperformed on positive stock selection in utilities and out of benchmark holdings in U.S. railroads. Sub-adviser Cohen & Steers (CNS) outperformed due to positive stock selection in utilities and oil and gas infrastructure. Sub-adviser KBI Global Investors underperformed as renewable utilities lagged. Sub-adviser Eagle Global Advisors underperformed on poor stock selection in oil and gas infrastructure.
