data.title: IAUI: A Hold For 2026 As Gold Rally Slows
data.description:
High-yield income funds are all in vogue now, and the NEOS Gold High Income ETF (IAUI) is a new addition this year. IAUI offers exposure to gold and a monthly distribution averaging $0.54 since inception. This adds up to an annualized yield of around 12%.

A common feature of income funds is that the options overlay strategy caps gains. This can be a major disadvantage during strong rallies like gold (IAU) had from August to October. However, the strategy shines during slower consolidation phases, and I think gold is in one now. This article looks at why this could be a good time to hold IAUI.

IAUI Basics

IAUI was launched by NEOS in June 2025. NEOS tends to find a reasonable balance of capital appreciation and income in its income ETFs and won an award for best options strategies ETF issuer.

The fund seeks to generate high monthly income through selling covered calls. This premium is then distributed monthly. A fairly steady distribution is paid out, and since gold has appreciated, so has the payment.

Note that because IAUI has only paid out for half a year, its currently listed TTM yield is half. After a full 12 months, it should be around 12-13%

Distributions are partially classed as return of capital for tax reasons, and the fund page states,

Distributions made by the Fund have been classified as a return of capital and may be comprised of option premiums, dividends, capital gains, and interest payments. As of the most recent distribution by the Fund, 92% was estimated to be return of capital. Please see the 19a-1 notices for a more comprehensive breakdown.

Note, the ROC is not eroding the NAV. The fund can declare a loss on the options even though they are not strictly a loss, as they are covered by gains in the underlying holdings.

When possible, they [management] seek to find losses in each ETF’s portfolio that can be used to offset gains, potentially helping a portion of Fund distributions receive a return of capital designation.

IAUI also provides the potential for capital appreciation based on exposure to exchange-traded products (“ETPs”) that have direct exposure to gold. The upside is capped due to the options, but that is a feature of all covered call strategies. Its holdings list looks like this -

The treasuries are just a cash alternative and collateral for the derivatives.

Since IAUI is actively managed, the options can be traded and changed so that a favorable income/capital appreciation balance is achieved. The expense fee is 0.78%, which is average for active management and average compared to its peers.

While these are all income funds based on gold, there are some major differences in yields and structure. Some have extremely high yields but less participation in rallies, while others are at the other end of the spectrum. Making a performance comparison may not be fair, as IAUI has yet to trade for 6 months and has yet to be tested under all market conditions.

IAUI's closest peers are GLDI and IGLD, and it's encouraging to see its total return compare favorably. GLDI and IGLD have been trading since 2021, so they have a proven track record.

Outperforming Gold

There's a misconception that income funds always underperform the underlying asset, but that really depends on the structure of the fund and the price action of the underlying.

Gold tends to experience long consolidation phases followed by strong break-outs and rallies. The last resting period happened in April-August, which saw gold trade in a contracting triangle.

IAUI outperformed IAU slightly during this period.

It has also outperformed IAU since the October peak as gold appears to be forming another consolidation.

Unfortunately, volatility also falls during these consolidation periods, and that means the income from options will be lower.

.

Still, IAUI should be able to provide a slight outperformance during consolidation phases. Furthermore, the drawdowns should be less, and of course, the steady income is a major plus.

On the downside, should gold break out strongly and repeat the August-October rally, IAUI will be left behind. IAUI has underperformed IAU by 7% since inception.

I don't want to leave 7% on the table, but this is a narrower underperformance than many other funds experience, and it's something I can tolerate. In any case, I am doubtful gold will repeat 2025's performance, which has been unusually strong.

While I am bullish, I'm not that bullish. I think there will be a rest in the $3800-$4300 area for several more months, in which time I would much rather hold IAUI than IAU.

Risks

IAUI is a new fund and does not have a proven track record. It may underperform similar ETFs in the longer term.

IAUI has not traded under a gold correction or bear market. In theory, it should slightly outperform during drawdowns, and the distributions should remain fairly constant, but that is not a guarantee.

Conclusions

IAUI has a good balance between high-income and capital appreciation and can outperform IAU during consolidation phases and minor drawdowns.

I think IAUI could be a good choice into early 2026, as gold looks to be entering an extended sideways period to consolidate the strong 2025 gains. If and when it does rally again, IAUI will still provide exposure and, of course, income. I rate it a buy.

data.title: Ford: Strong YTD Rally, But Future Returns Likely To Track The Market
data.description:
Ford (F) is an American automotive heavyweight that appears to be experiencing a significant improvement in the fundamentals, highlighting an accelerating revenue growth and a streak of five consecutive double-beat quarters. The stock is up 31% on a year-to-date basis and has significantly outperformed the benchmark.

The company trades at roughly 12x earnings multiple, implying a 27% discount versus peers at approximately 17x. Ford offers a 5.85% dividend yield, which seems to be an appealing bonus to an undervalued name. Although it may appear to be a compelling opportunity for long-term investors seeking dividend income, it hasn't grown dividends over the past year, and is heavily leveraged with $164 billion in total obligations.

I think the stock may consolidate after a significant rally. The current valuation implies a multi-year high, which also signals that upside may be limited in the near term. It could appear a compelling diversification in the consumer discretionary space if bottom-line growth delivers and shareholder-friendly approach continues.

However, I wouldn't be surprised if the automotive company experiences a profit-taking after a sharp upside move, which would support my neutral stance. I rate Ford a Hold with a $13.70 price target. This presents a 5% upside possibility without dividends, and roughly market-like performance with yield. The company appears to be a well-known name in the space, but suppressed margins and heavy leverage cloud the future outlook, especially if the Fed decides to pause the easing cycle.

Turnaround Remains In Progress

On October 23, Ford delivered FQ3 2025 results. This was the fifth consecutive double-beat quarterly performance, indicating improving fundamentals and a solid market position. The market reacted to the results with a 12% upside over the following trading day.

The automotive company posted $50.5 billion in revenue, implying a nearly 10% revenue growth, and $3.32 billion above what the market had anticipated. The bottom-line arrived at $0.45 in diluted EPS, a roughly 10% decline on a year-over-year basis versus $0.49 in the same quarter last year, but $0.09 above analysts' estimates. The company experienced significant revenue growth, which contributed to the improved investor confidence.

In the earnings presentation, management underscored that it has achieved $4.3 billion in adjusted free cash flow, highlighting a $1.1 billion increase on a year-over-year basis.

The company's top-line performance was driven by the segment of Model E, unlocking a new revenue growth cycle tied to EV and hybrid vehicles. Although the category achieved an EBIT loss of $1.4 billion, this remains reasonable and justified by expansion, indicating new product introduction in Europe. This may drag on profitability in the short term, but it remains a long-term tailwind for the automotive company. Given this, F appears to be more diversified, which may appear to be a compelling trait for long-term investors.

To support this, the global EV market was estimated at $1.3 trillion in 2024 and is expected to quadruple by 2030, reaching $6.5 trillion. This represents a 32.5% CAGR and validates the company's pivot to the EV space. If the company can continue growing the segment at a double-digit pace, it could significantly benefit from broader market growth.

The Ford Blue segment generated $1.5 billion in EBIT, reaching a 5.5% EBIT margin. The Ford Pro led the performance with $2 billion in EBIT contribution, resulting in 11.4%. The automotive name continues experiencing solid momentum in demand.

The Ford Blue remained the largest segment, indicating $28 billion in revenue, nearly a 7% increase on a year-over-year basis. Ford Pro posted $17.4 billion in top-line contribution, an 11% surge over the past 12 months, and the Model E category was the fastest growing with a 50% surge, reaching $1.8 billion in revenue. It seems that the automotive company is expanding across all cylinders, which is supporting a bullish stance, reflecting a strengthening moat.

Although the company's performance is impacted by tariffs, it continues to improve FCF. Management announced this was the fifth consecutive quarter, reflecting a reduction in costs, despite the tariff impact. This argues that Ford is well-managed and supports a positive outlook. The company has also announced a Q4 regular dividend of $0.15, continuing a shareholder-friendly capital allocation approach.

To conclude, the automotive company remains a dominant player in the space. Ford experiences seven straight months of US sales growth, justifying a strong momentum in demand and improving results. The company's pivot to the EV landscape is gaining attention, and a 50% increase in revenue reflects a solid initial demand. Although Ford is impacted by tariffs, it continues improving FCF, and that fuels a bullish narrative.

However, the automotive company remains heavily leveraged with $164 billion in total obligations. This resulted in $1.221 billion in interest expense over the past 12 months. In addition to this, EV product expansion may drag on the profitability metrics, clouding the near-term outlook. The EV space remains highly competitive, and execution risks remain. If Ford can scale into the space successfully, the market may be willing to reward the automotive company with a higher premium, but struggling to succeed may result in a lower valuation.

I think a significant stock price rally may take a breather. I wouldn't be surprised if it consolidates until management disperses uncertainty tied to future demand and pressured margins. Ford may have unlocked a new growth cycle with the Model E segment, but the recent rally may have partially priced in future revenue growth; thus, I remain cautious.

Ford Remains Undervalued

The automotive heavyweight trades at a forward P/E of about 12x, which suggests a 27% discount versus peers at 17x. The company appears to be trading at roughly 90% undervaluation versus the average company in the S&P500 at about 23x earnings multiple.

Although this may argue for bullish continuation from undervalued levels, the chart below suggests the earnings multiple expanded by 2x since the beginning of the year, and the current ratio stands at a multi-year high. Even though the stock may appreciate further, I wouldn't be surprised if Ford consolidated after a sharp swing.

On a forward EV/Sales and Price/Sales ratios of 1.04 and 0.29, respectively, the company trades at roughly a 40% discount versus peers at 1.29 and 0.89. This highlights Ford being undervalued and argues for continued bullish momentum. However, the forward EV/EBITDA and EV/EBIT at 17.97 and 28.48, respectively, offset the bullish narrative and represent a 70% overvaluation at the midpoint versus sector medians of 10.15 and 15.72. This supports my neutral stance, highlighting a mixed picture.

Over the past 12 months, Ford has grown its revenue by 3.75%, implying a 13% advantage versus peers at 3.31%. In addition to this, based on the recent quarterly performance, reflecting accelerated revenue growth, I believe the automotive company may achieve a more rapid top-line surge. This is also supported by EV and hybrid vehicle expansion.

The bottom line increased by 33% over the past 12 months, a significant outperformance versus peers at 1%. Highlighting the improving fundamentals, indicating a cost efficiency strategy and an increase in FCF, I think Ford may be able to continue growing diluted EPS further. If F sustains a solid high-single-digit growth or can deliver a double-digit performance, there is room for multiple expansion.

Furthermore, Ford operates at suppressed margins, and with the EV and hybrid pivot, may continue weighing on the profitability. The company operates at a gross profit margin (TTM) of 7.55% and a net income margin (TTM) of 2.48%. On the upside, F generated $20.43 billion in cash from operations (TTM), reflecting a 7x outperformance versus peers at $299 million.

I think Ford's valuation presents a mixed picture. It continues trading at a discounted valuation versus the sector, but the recent surge represents a multi-year premium earnings multiple. There remains a risk of profit-taking, especially if consumer spending weakens, which could result in Ford trading closer to its business typical valuation.

Nevertheless, the automotive company continues to successfully apply cost optimization strategies, resulting in FCF growth. Ford is also tied to EV tailwinds, which could drive future revenue expansion. Although it may temporarily stress profitability metrics, the broader revenue growth picture remains intact. The company's high leverage appears concerning, but with positive FCF, it could be able to continue proceeding with a shareholder-friendly capital allocation approach through dividends and reducing its debt.

Wall Street aims for $1.44 in diluted EPS for FY2026. I agree with the estimate, supported by improving fundamentals and solid momentum in demand. If the economy doesn't enter a recession, F may be able to achieve a higher target than this. However, I also think it may consolidate after a double-digit surge, and earnings multiple may contract closer to business typical levels.

Therefore, if we apply the $1.44 diluted EPS estimate of FY2026 to a 9.5x earnings multiple, we arrive at my price target of $13.70. This presents a 5% upside possibility without dividends and roughly market-like performance. Although I believe the turnaround story remains in progress, the market may want to see whether the company's pivot to the EV and hybrid space succeeds. It weighs on profitability, which could impact bottom-line expansion in the following quarters, but the broader revenue growth picture remains intact. I believe, if F succeeds and continues reporting double-digit surges in sales growth, the market may be willing to reprice the stock at a higher premium. On the other hand, any uncertainty tied to outlook may cap upside in the near term.

Main Risks And Concerns

There are risks and concerns tied to Ford that could meaningfully impact stock price performance in the short term.

To begin with, the company is expanding its Model E segment, but it comes with execution risk. The global EV market is expected to significantly grow over the following years, but this also comes with fierce competition. US, European, and Asian companies are competing in the space, and it may be a challenging environment to gain a significant market share in the EV space.

Furthermore, the automotive industry remains capital-intensive, and this indicates a heavily leveraged capital structure, which is a concern. Although Ford can achieve improvements in FCF, investors should monitor whether the automotive brand can sustain the progress. If results weaken, I think F may contract to the business's typical earnings multiple.

I also think that after a recent rally, there may be a lack of revenue growth catalysts that could support continued price action, resulting in broader market outperformance. It appears that investors have priced in cost optimization and solid momentum in EV and hybrid demand. Unless Ford unlocks new catalysts, it appears likely to trade in a range.

On the other hand, if Ford delivers high-single-digit or double-digit revenue growth further, it remains well-positioned to benefit from strong momentum demand. The company is tied to EV-powered tailwinds, but investors should monitor whether EV and hybrid expansion will be successful.

Conclusion

I think Ford remains a compelling diversification candidate for investors seeking expanded exposure to the automotive industry. The company has an appealing brand, a long history, and offers dividend income, which may come as a compelling bonus to an undervalued name.

The company's pivot to the EV and hybrid space has been successful so far. A 50% surge in revenue on a year-over-year basis appears impressive, but investors should remain cautious due to significant competition in the space. I also think this pivot is capital-intensive and may continue pressuring profitability performance. Although the stock remains undervalued, after a recent surge, it trades at multi-year highs, which may suggest a breather and consolidation.

I think Ford remains an interesting opportunity, but I also argue it is unlikely to outperform the benchmark over the following year. I rate Ford a Hold with a $13.70 price target, representing a 5% upside possibility without dividends. 22 Wall Street analysts forecast a $12.50 price target over the next year, signaling 3% downside risk. I am feeling more bullish, as I believe F deserves a higher premium due to its strong moat, brand presence, and long history. Time will tell, but it will be interesting to see whether expectations deliver.

data.title: EZA- Cheaply Valued, And In Dazzling Form, But Don't Get Complacent
data.description:
Solid Alpha

It’s been well-documented that 2025 has been a relatively good year for emerging markets, with the iShares MSCI Emerging Markets ETF (EEM) generating ample returns of 30%, 1.5x more than global stocks. If you thought 30% returns were good enough for a diversified ETF vehicle, how should one eulogize equities from Africa’s largest economy, where the iShares MSCI South Africa ETF (EZA) has generated exceptional returns of 60%, which is double what EMs in general have managed?

Interestingly enough, despite the stellar gains, EZA, even as recently as this week, is still being bandied around as one of the more promising non-US ETFs plays for FY26.

So, if EZA’s ongoing halo has managed to garner your attention, and you’d like to better understand this ETF, and explore a potential position in the same, here are a few subplots worth considering.

Lone Man Standing, Although Diversified Options Exist

Before we expand on EZA’s good and bad qualities, it is worth noting that it is the only US-listed ETF that offers focused exposure to South African equities; that wasn’t the case until November 2023, when another alternative, the Franklin FTSE South Africa ETF (FLZA), was still in existence (FLZA eventually got liquidated on the 16 th of November 2023). Of course, if investors want a bit more diversification beyond just South African equities, they could always pursue the VanEck Africa Index (AFK), where close to two-thirds of the portfolio comes from non-SA African stocks, with South African stocks still dominating.

EZA ETF Characteristics- The Good and The Bad

Nonetheless, coming back to EZA, it has been around for 23 years now, during which it has managed to amass total AUM [assets under management] of over $560M. EZA, primarily seeks to deliver returns by tracking the MSCI South Africa 25/50 Index [MS25I], a proxy of the top 85% of South Africa’s market-cap, and consists only of 27 large- and mid-cap stocks.

However, despite having the objective of covering only a narrow pool of stocks (27) of the MS25I, EZA doesn’t do a particularly great job of mirroring the performance of its tracking index.

This is reflected in the very wide tracking error readings of 14-16% across different durations. We feel risk-averse investors too ought to be wary of pursuing EZA, as the variability of its returns tends to be a lot more spread out than a typical ETF (see standard deviation and annualized volatility readings, which are well above the ETF norm).

However, one area where EZA scores very highly is on the dividend front, where you can pocket a hefty yield of over 4.6%, which is almost 200 bps better than what most ETFs yield. Note, however, that its dividend growth trajectory is quite haphazard; over a 5-year basis, dividends have contracted, but on a trailing twelve-month basis, it is growing at an exceptional pace of triple-digit-percentages

We believe that EZA can afford to dole out such lucrative yields because it is heavily dominated by two dividend-oriented sectors- materials and financials—that jointly account for over two-thirds of the portfolio. While the financials help in covering consistent dividends over the years, material stocks are generally responsible for driving lucrative but inconsistent payouts, much of which is linked to the volatile commodity cycle, which tends to go through boom-and-bust phases.

Besides that, one intriguing quirk of EZA is that its top holding (a sizeable individual weight of 14%)- Naspers Limited (OTCPK:NPSNY)—is more of a global consumer internet and technology holding company, whose fortunes are not overwhelmingly linked to the South African economy- far from it. Rather, only 15% of its overall revenue comes from the African continent as a whole, with business largely coming from Eastern Europe and Latin America.

Long-standing observers of NPSNY may also argue that much of NPSNY’s allure comes from its elevated stake (a 23.5% stake) in the Chinese tech conglomerate- Tencent (OTCPK:TCEHY). However, NPSNY (through its subsidiary Prosus) is currently in the process of gradually paring down it stake in TCEHY, and using that to buy back its own stock, which may appear to be a questionable strategy of capital allocation to a fair few investors.

How Is EZA Currently Positioned Relative to Its EM Peers?

Despite EZA’s meteoric rise this year, note that EZA’s relative strength ratio vs a diversified emerging market pocket is still trading at a 9% discount to its long-term average of 1.37x.

One could make an argument for further mean reversion in favor of South African equities, as their valuations still look relatively cheap vs. EMs, particularly in light of the superior earnings growth they are set to deliver. Basically, EZA is priced at an earnings multiple of 10x, whereas EMs are almost priced at a 50% premium of close to 15x. Listed equity financial products are typically priced at a premium when their underlying holdings are poised to deliver superior growth, but that doesn’t appear to be the case here, with EMs on course to deliver long-term earnings of less than 11%, whereas EZA’s holdings are poised to deliver a superior cadence of earnings growth of over 300 bps.

Closing Thoughts - What Do EZA’s Charts Suggest?

Momentum players are currently likely taking to EZA like moths to a flame, as it is witnessing a series of higher highs and higher lows and is trading above all its key moving averages of 50, 100, and 200 on the daily chart. However, we would urge investors not to get too carried away at this juncture, as EZA has now reached a dicey zone, which has previously served as a hindrance on the long-term charts.

As you can see from the image above, we have seen three separate instances (highlighted in yellow) where EZA has approached the $65-$72 levels, only to be faced with a spike of supply in the shares, resulting in a sell-off. Note also that the price is currently perched outside the upper Bollinger band, which is a reflection of overextension, as it is two standard deviations above the 20-month rolling average. Supplementing this upward overextension, we also have the monthly RSI indicator at the lower end of the chart, which has now breached the overbought zone. The last time the RSI hit similar levels was way back in 2007, after which EZA ended up witnessing a contraction in its market-cap by roughly two-thirds, which took place over a 1-year time frame!

We are not suggesting that one should brace for a similar degree of selling pressure (particularly when EZA’s valuations and yields still look attractive), but it is certainly worth pondering if we are not too far away from an intermediate top. All in all, we don’t believe it would pay to be too bullish at this juncture, and prefer to go with a hold rating.

data.title: PGIM Quant Solutions Mid-Cap Value Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Review

Market volatility persisted during the third quarter, driven by lingering tariff concerns, inflation, and geopolitical instability. Nevertheless, U.S. equity markets continued to surge, with the S&P 500 advancing over 8% during the quarter.

Equity markets continued to set record highs during the quarter, largely fueled by the artificial intelligence (AI) boom. However, gains were uneven, with weak earnings, reduced guidance, and sector rotations weighing on many names. In September, the Federal Reserve (Fed) announced its first rate cut of the year, cutting its benchmark interest rate by 0.25%, citing slowing job growth, a cooling labor market, and slowing economic growth. Looking ahead, many investors expect an additional one to two rate cuts before year-end.

Bullish investors remain focused on the strength and resilience of the U.S. economy, additional rate cuts, and the continued technological innovation driven by AI that is likely to fuel growth across numerous industries.

More bearish investors remain concerned about policy uncertainty, geopolitical instability, elevated valuations, and lingering tariff concerns risks.

Within the mid-cap space, value stocks outperformed growth stocks for the quarter (Russell Mid-cap Value Index: 6.18% versus Russell Mid-cap Growth Index: 2.78%).

Quarter

The PGIM Quant Solutions Mid-Cap Value Fund outperformed its Index, the Russell Mid-Cap Value Index (the Index), on a gross basis, in the third quarter of 2025.

U.S. equity markets extended their rally after the market's swoon in early 2025 with all of the major U.S. indexes finishing firmly in positive territory. While growth stocks outperformed among large-cap stocks by a wide margin, value stocks performed better in the mid-cap and small-cap space. Indeed, the small cap Russell 2000 was the quarter's big winner by far, with both growth and value stocks advancing by more than 12%. With value stocks performing well among mid-cap stocks, the Fund outperformed during the quarter as the Fund's valuation factors skewed positively while our business prospect factors were only modestly productive.

The Fund outperformed the Index in six of the eleven sectors for the quarter.

Contributors to performance included Industrial and Real Estate stocks. The Fund benefited from strong selection among Construction & Engineering and Professional Services stocks, which generally benefited from infrastructure spending related to the development of AI development. In Real Estate, the Fund's underweight exposure to this underperforming sector coupled with good selection supported performance.

Utility and Information Technology (IT) stocks detracted from the Fund's performance. In Utilities, underperformance stemmed from poor selection and an underweight position in Electric Utilities, which generally performed well during the quarter. Among Information Technology stocks, the Fund suffered from poor selection among IT Services stocks, particularly in the Internet Services and Infrastructure industry.

OUTLOOK & POSITIONING

Investors in US equity markets can be forgiven for forgetting the ear-year rapid and sizeable correction as stocks roared back into firmly positive territory following the second and third quarter's powerful rally. After stabilizing in the first quarter, growth stocks re-established their dominance over value into the third quarter, especially among large caps. The outperformance of large-cap relative to small-cap stocks also re-emerged, although the gap in performance was not as wide as in previous years.

The market's big rally off its lows was driven largely by several key developments. The tariff threat from the Trump administration which roiled markets early in the year receded as negotiations brought many of the penalties to much more manageable levels. The Fed moved off its "wait and see" approach to interest rates and reduced its key Federal-Funds rate by 25 basis points and signaled it might make several more cuts in future periods. Spending on AI large language models continued to increase dramatically, expanding into real estate development, construction and power development. Together, these items and others encouraged significant flows into U.S. and global equity markets.

From a broader perspective, while the market's nosedive and subsequent rapid recovery have made investors breathe a bit easier, we would continue to be cautious about signaling the "all clear" on other potential risks. Earnings growth has been strong but has been limited to only a few sectors. At the same time, valuations have become even more stretched, reaching historically high levels, especially among larger cap indexes. We are also seeing some signs of growing speculative activity, with the return of "meme stocks" and outperformance by shares in companies with no or negative earnings. And while the Fed has stepped in to offset some of the weakness emerging in the jobs market, their intervention may be limited by persistent inflation. Given the risk backdrop, we continue to anticipate more moderate returns and heightened volatility in coming quarters. Within this context, we favor cheaper rather than more expensive stocks, given their still deeply discounted valuations, and we believe that the Fund is well positioned to benefit from this dynamic.
